## STABILIZED MEDICAL IMAGE ATTACKS

## **Gege Qi**[1] **, Lijun Gong**[1] _[∗]_ **, Yibing Song**[2] _[∗]_ **, Kai Ma**[1] **, Yefeng Zheng**[1]

1 Tencent Jarvis Lab, Shenzhen, China

2 Tencent AI Lab, Shenzhen, China

lijungong@tencent.com yibingsong.cv@gmail.com yefengzheng@tencent.com

## ABSTRACT

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have advanced existing medical systems for automatic disease diagnosis. However, a threat to these systems arises that adversarial attacks make CNNs vulnerable. Inaccurate diagnosis results make a negative influence on human healthcare. There is a need to investigate potential adversarial attacks to robustify deep medical diagnosis systems. On the other side, there are several modalities of medical images (e.g., CT, fundus, and endoscopic image) of which each type is significantly different from others. It is more challenging to generate adversarial perturbations for different types of medical images. In this paper, we propose an image-based medical adversarial attack method to consistently produce adversarial perturbations on medical images. The objective function of our method consists of a loss deviation term and a loss stabilization term. The loss deviation term increases the divergence between the CNN prediction of an adversarial example and its ground truth label. Meanwhile, the loss stabilization term ensures similar CNN predictions of this example and its smoothed input. From the perspective of the whole iterations for perturbation generation, the proposed loss stabilization term exhaustively searches the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape. We further analyze the KL-divergence of the proposed loss function and find that the loss stabilization term makes the perturbations updated towards a fixed objective spot while deviating from the ground truth. This stabilization ensures the proposed medical attack effective for different types of medical images while producing perturbations in small variance. Experiments on several medical image analysis benchmarks including the recent COVID-19 dataset show the stability of the proposed method.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Computer Aided Diagnosis (CADx) has been widely applied in the medical screening process. The automatic diagnosis benefits doctors to efficiently obtain health status to avoid disease exacerbation. Recently, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been utilized in CADx to improve the diagnosis accuracy. The discriminative representations improve the performance of medical image analysis including lesion localization, segmentation and disease classification. However, recent advances in adversarial examples have revealed that the deployed CADx systems are usually fragile to adversarial attacks (Finlayson et al., 2019), e.g., small perturbations applied to the input images can deceive CNNs to have opposite conclusions. As mentioned in Ma et al. (2020), the vast amount of money in the healthcare economy may attract attackers to commit insurance fraud or false claims of medical reimbursement by manipulating medical reports. Moreover, image noise is a common issue during the data collection process and sometimes these noise perturbations could implicitly form adversarial attacks. For example, particle contamination of optical lens in dermoscopy and endoscopy and metal/respiratory artifacts of CT scans frequently deteriorate the quality of collected images. Therefore, there is a growing interest to investigate how medical diagnosis systems respond to adversarial attacks and what we can do to improve the robustness of the deployed systems.
While recent studies of adversarial attacks mainly focus on natural images, the research of adversarial attacks in the medical image domain is desired as there are significant differences between

two domains. Beyond regular RGB cameras, there are various types of medical imaging equipments (e.g., Computed Tomography (CT) scanners, ultrasound transducers and fundus cameras) to generate dramatically different images. Fig. 1 shows three examples where an image captured from fundus camera is in (a), an image captured from the CT scanner is in (e) and an endoscopic video frame is in (i). As can be seen in the figure that these three images have little in common. The huge data variance across different modalities of medical images brings more challenges to develop a technology that works for all the modalities. In addition, existing investigations on medical adversarial attacks are limited. In Finlayson et al. (2019), adversarial examples are shown to deteriorate the diagnosis accuracy of deep learning based medical systems. These medical attack methods are mainly based on those from natural images (e.g., Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) (Goodfellow et al., 2014) and Project Gradient Descent (PGD) (Madry et al., 2017), which are insufficiently developed for different types of medical data. As shown in Fig. 1, the adversarial examples generated by FGSM and PGD do not consistently decrease the network’s performance in (b), (c), (f), (g), (j) and (k). The data variance in (a) and (e) leads to the inconsistent attack results by existing methods.

In this paper, we propose a medical image attack method to consistently produce adversarial perturbations that can fool deep medical diagnosis systems working with different medical data modalities. The perturbations are iteratively generated via taking partial derivatives of a well-defined objective function that is composed of a deviation loss term and a stabilized loss term with respect to the input. By maximizing the deviation loss term, the adversarial attack system enlarges the divergence between CNN predictions and the ground truth to have effective attack samples. To handle the aforementioned ubiquitous data noise issue in medical images, we propose a novel stabilization loss term as an extra regularization, which ensures a consistent deviation trajectory for the crafted attack samples. Meanwhile, the stabilization term avoids the local optima in the optimization process caused by the image noise.

The proposed stabilization loss term is designed to measure the difference between two CNN predictions, where the first prediction is from the crafted adversarial sample and the second one is from the same sample processed with a Gaussian smoothing. Given an adversarial example _A_ and its Gaussian smoothed result _A_[�] , the loss stabilization term constrains the corresponding CNN predictions (i.e., _f_ ( _A_ ) and _f_ ( _A_[�] )) to be similar via a minimization process. The intuition from the scale space optimization (Lindeberg, 1992) indicates that the minimization of _f_ ( _A_ ) and _f_ ( _A_[�] ) will exhaustively search the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape. We further analyze this stabilized loss term via KL-divergence and find that the CNN predictions are steered towards a fixed objective spot during iterations. This stabilization improves the attack effectiveness on different types of medical data including CT, fundus, and endoscopic images. We evaluate the proposed Stablized Medical Image Attack (SMIA) on several medical datasets (APT, 2019; EAD, 2019; Kag, 2015), including the recent COVID-19 (COV, 2019) lung CT. Thorough evaluations demonstrate that the proposed method is effective to produce perturbations that decrease the prediction accuracy of different medical diagnosis systems. Our investigation provides a guidance for strengthening the robustness of these medical systems towards adversarial attacks.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In this section, we literately review existing adversarial attack methods on both natural and medical images. Meanwhile, we survey relevant medical image analysis tasks where SMIA is deployed.

## 2.1 ADVERSARIAL ATTACK

There are extensive investigations on adversarial attacks for natural image classifications. In Goodfellow et al. (2014), FGSM was proposed to generate adversarial examples based on the CNN gradients. A DeepFool method was proposed in Moosavi-Dezfooli et al. (2016) to compute minimal perturbations based on classifier’s linearization. In Moosavi-Dezfooli et al. (2017), an iterative algorithm was proposed to generate perturbations and showed the existence of a universal (imageagnostic) adversarial perturbations. In Baluja & Fischer (2017), a Transformation Network (ATN) was trained to generate adversarial examples without gradient involvement. The adversarial training and provable defense were proposed in Balunovic & Vechev (2020) to achieve both attack robustness and high accuracy. The capsule based reconstructive attack was proposed in Qin et al. (2020) to cause both misclassifications and reconstructive errors. Besides image classification, several attack methods were proposed for semantic segmentation, object detection and object tracking Jia et al. (2020). In Fischer et al. (2017); Dong et al. (2019), the classification based attacks were shown transferable to attack deep image segmentation results. The universal perturbations were demonstrated existing in Moosavi-Dezfooli et al. (2017). Moreover, a Dense Adversary Generation (DAG) method was proposed Xie et al. (2017) for both semantic segmentation and object detection attacks. The general idea of natural image attacks was to iteratively generate perturbations based on the CNN gradients to maximize the network predictions of adversarial examples and the ground truth labels. This idea was also reflected in Finlayson et al. (2019) to show the medical attacks. Different from existing methods, we propose a stabilized regularization term to ensure the consistent generation of adversarial perturbations, which are effective for different types of medical image datasets.

## 2.2 DEEP MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS

The deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been shown effective to automatically analyze medical images (Litjens et al., 2017; Razzak et al., 2018). The common CADx applications include classifying the stage of disease, the detection and segmentation of organs and lesions.

**Disease classification.** Most medical systems formulate disease diagnosis as an image classification task. The types of diseases are predefined and each type corresponds to one category. During the classification process, there are single or multiple images as input for disease diagnosis. In Shen et al. (2015), a multi-scale CNN was proposed to capture the feature representation of lung nodule patches for accurate classification. A multi-instance layer and a multi-scale layer were proposed in Li et al. (2019) to diagnose diabetic macular edema and myopic macular degeneration. Besides diagnosing the types of disease, existing medical systems were also able to predict the disease status (Gulshan et al., 2016) by empirical status categorization.

3

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**Organ and lesion detection.** The detection of organ and lesion is inspired by the object detection framework for natural images (e.g., Faster-RCNN (Ren et al., 2015), FPN (Lin et al., 2017), and Yolo (Redmon et al., 2016)). Besides, 3D spatial information of the medical data is explored in the 3D detection framework. In Ding et al. (2017), a 3D-CNN classification approach was proposed to classify lung nodule candidates that were previously detected by the Faster-RCNN detector. The RPN (Ren et al., 2015) was extended in Liao et al. (2019) to become 3D-RPN for 3D proposal generation to detect lung nodules. Different from the 3D detection framework, a multi-scale booster was proposed in Shao et al. (2019) with channel and spatial attentions that were integrated into FPN for suspicious lesion detection in 2D CT slices. The detection methods based on 2D image input reduced heavy computational cost brought in the 3D inputs for 3D detection methods.

**Organ and lesion segmentation.** The medical segmentation was significantly advanced via deep encoder-decoder structures (e.g., U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015)). This architecture contains a contracting path (i.e., encoder) to capture global context and a symmetric expanding path (i.e., decoder) to obtain precise localization. There were several works built upon U-Net. In Brosch et al. (2016), skip connections were utilized in the first and last convolutional layers to segment lesions in brain. A V-Net was proposed in Milletari et al. (2016) to segment brain’s anatomical structures. It followed 3D U-Net structure consisting of 3D convolutional layers. A dual pathway and multi-scale 3D-CNN architecture was proposed in Kamnitsas et al. (2017) to generate both global and local lesion representations in the CNN for brain lesion segmentation. Existing medical methods mainly utilized deep encoder-decoder architectures for end-to-end segmentation of organs and lesions.

As illustrated above, deep medical diagnosis systems differ much from the CNN architectures that are developed for natural images. Moreover, the variance of different modalities of medical images is significantly larger than that of natural images. Therefore, the adversarial attacks designed for natural images are not often effective in the medical domain. Nevertheless, the limitations that are brought by huge network and data variance are effectively solved via our stabilized medical attack.

## 3 PROPOSED METHOD

In this section, we illustrate the details of medical image attacks. We first show the objective function of SMIA that consists of a loss deviation term and a loss stabilization term. The loss deviation term produces the perturbation to decrease the image analysis performance, while the loss stabilization term is consistently updated during iterations and constrains these perturbations to low variance. Then, we analyze how SMIA affects the generated perturbations during iterative optimization from the perspective of KL-divergence. The analysis is followed by a visualization showing the variance and cosine distance of perturbations by utilizing the loss stabilization term.

## 3.3 VISUALIZATIONS

The analysis on _L_ STA from KL-divergence indicates that the iteratively generated perturbations are in small variance and updated in a more consistent direction. We visualize the perturbations in different iterations in Fig. 2 to see how perturbations vary in practice. We send an input fundus image to the ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) integrated with a graph convolutional network (Kipf & Welling, 2016) framework for image classification. The perturbations are generated via Eq. 1 and Eq. 4 independently.

Fig. 2 shows the visual comparison of perturbations generated on the first, fourth, and ninth iterations. We denote the perturbations generated via _L_ DEV (i.e., Eq. 1) as _P_ DEV and that generated via _L_ DEV + _L_ STA (i.e., Eq. 4) as _P_ DEV+STA. The visualization indicates that at the same iteration, the variance of _P_ DEV is larger than that of _P_ DEV+STA. Meanwhile, during different iterations, there are severe variance oscillations in _P_ DEV while _P_ DEV+STA is consistently decreased. On the other side, we calculate the cosine distance between _P_ DEV and _P_ DEV+STA separately. The cosine distance indicates that _P_ DEV is updated in diverse directions while _P_ DEV+STA is more consistent. The visualization accords with the KL-divergence analysis that the proposed loss stabilization term decreases the perturbation variance and constrain a stable update direction towards a fixed objective spot in the CNN feature space.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the proposed method on three medical image analysis tasks including diabetic retinopathy grading, artefact detection, and lung segmentation. The diabetic retinopathy grading is to classify fundus images into predefined categories for diabetes status estimation. The artefact detection is to detect specific artefacts like pixel saturations, motion blur, and specular reflections in the endoscopic images. Lung segmentation is to segment lung region from the whole CT slice. The medical data in one task is significantly different from that in others.

We use two datasets for diabetic retinopathy grading. One is the APTOS-2019 (APT, 2019) dataset with 3,662 fundus images. The other is a large-scale Kaggle-DR (Kag, 2015) dataset where we randomly select 11,000 fundus images from its original training set. Both APTOS-2019 and KaggleDR contains five defined categories. For artefact detection we use EAD-2019 (EAD, 2019) dataset with 2,500 images collected from endoscopic video frames and annotated artefact regions with seven defined categories. These detection images focus on multiple image modalities (i.e., gastroscopy, cystoscopy, gastro-oesophageal and colonoscopy), and are captured in multi-resolution with multimodal (i.e., white light, fluorescence, and narrow band imaging). For lung segmentation, we use the COVID-19 dataset (COV, 2019) where there are 20 CT scans for lungs infected by COVID-19.

In practice, we use ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) integrated with graph convolutional network (Kipf & Welling, 2016) to classify fundus images, the multi-scale booster framework (Shao et al., 2019) to detect artefact, and U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) to segment infected lung regions. The proposed method is compared to other adversarial attack methods including FGSM (Goodfellow et al., 2014), PGD (Madry et al., 2017), DeepFool (Moosavi-Dezfooli et al., 2016), and DAG (Xie et al., 2017). The comparisons are made by adding these generated perturbations on the input images for decreased performance observation. For evaluation metrics, we use mean accuracy for medical image classification, IoU and mean Average Precision (mAP) for detection, and mean accuracy and Jaccard index for segmentation. During perturbation generation, we set _ϵ_ as 0 _._ 05, 0 _._ 01, 5 _×_ 10 _[−]_[5] for lung segmentation, artefact detection and diabetic retinopathy grading respectively. We stop at the 10-th iteration for all the attack methods. The _α_ in Eq. 4 is set as 1. We provide more results including using different Gaussian kernels _W_ and show the pseudo code in the supplementary files. Our implementation will be made available to the public.

Table 1 shows the ablation study results on three datasets. We denote “Clean” as the results generated by medical diagnosis systems without adding perturbations. These results indicate the upper limit of these systems. In general, the perturbations generated via deviation loss term (DEV) decrease the recognition performance (i.e., segmentation, detection, and classification results in COVID-19, EAD-2019, and ATPOS-2019, respectively). By integrating loss stabilization term (STA), we are able to generate perturbations that decrease the recognition performance even further. On the other hand, the percentage of positive cosine distance values of DEV+STA is higher than DEV, which indicates the perturbations generated via DEV+STA are more consistent with each other during their iterative generation process. The results shown in this table indicate that STA improves the adversarial attack with DEV alone on both performance deterioration and perturbation consistency on three medical analysis scenarios.

Next, we analyze how the hyper-parameters of existing attack methods affect the attack performance on the EAD-2019 dataset. Tables 2-4 show the analysis results. We evaluate attack performance by using different iteration numbers in Table 2, different values of _ϵ_ in Table 3, and different values of _α_ in Table 4, respectively. The analysis on iteration numbers indicates that existing attack methods decrease the artefact detection performance by increasing the iteration numbers. SMIA significantly decreases the performance by using a limited number of iterations. The analysis on _ϵ_ shows that SMIA performs favorably against existing methods when it is set as 0.01. Besides, SMIA decreases most when _α_ = 1. The hyper-parameter analysis shows our optimal parameter selections.

## 4.2 COMPARISONS WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART

We compare the proposed SMIA method with existing adversarial attack methods in the same medical analysis scenarios. Besides the prevalent FGSM and PGD methods, we involve DAG which is

proposed for detection and segmentation of natural images, and DeepFool that is for disease image classification. All these methods are deployed on the medical diagnosis systems to show the recognition performance decrease. Meanwhile, we compute the variance of all the generated perturbations, which is shown as “Var” in the results.

Table 6 shows the evaluation performance. Under lung segmentation (i.e., COVID-19) and diabetic retinopathy grading (i.e., APTOS-2019 and Kaggle-DR) scenarios, our SMIA consistently decreases the recognition performance of medical systems. The drops in accuracy and Jaccard index brought by our method are more than those from existing methods. Specifically, the accuracy drop on the COVID-19 dataset brought by our method is around 9% more than the second best method (i.e., DAG). Moreover, the accuracy drop by using our method on the APTOS-19 dataset is around 10% more than the second best method (i.e., PGD). The false positive rate brought by our method is higher than other methods in Kaggle-DR. These performance drops indicate the effectiveness of our method to handle medical image data. Under the artefact detection scenario in EAD-2019, we observe that the decrease of mAP from DAG is more than ours. This is because DAG synthesizes pseudo labels for each proposal from the detection network to specifically attack the classifier of the detector. Nevertheless, its performance decrease is not as significant as ours (i.e., 7.62% v.s. 4.8%) under the IoU metric that measures the bounding box overlap ratios. On the other hand, the perturbation variance (i.e., “Var”) of our method is lower than other attack methods on all the evaluation datasets. The decreased performance and smaller perturbations indicate the effectiveness of our SMIA method for attacking medical diagnosis systems.

Besides medical image datasets, we evaluate SMIA on a hand-written image dataset MNIST (Deng, 2012) with existing methods. Table 5 shows the results. Our SMIA method decreases the recognition accuracy more than that of FGSM and PGD. This indicates that SMIA is effective to attack not only medical images, but also images from other fields.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We proposed a medical image adversarial attack method to diagnose current medical systems. The proposed method iteratively generates adversarial perturbations by maximizing the deviation loss term and minimizing the loss stabilization term. For an adversarial example at the current iteration, the difference between its CNN prediction and the corresponding ground truth label is enlarged, while the CNN predictions of its smoothed input and itself are similar. The similar predictions constrain the perturbations to be generated in a relatively flat region in the CNN feature space. During consecutive iterations, the perturbations are updated from one flat region to another. Thus the proposed method can search the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape. Compared to the perturbation movement among single spots that are brought by only using loss deviation term, the loss stabilization term improves the attack performance by regularizing the perturbation movement to be stable. Further analysis with the KL-divergence shows that the minimization of loss stabilization term is to regularize the perturbations to move towards a fixed objective spot while their variances are reduced. Both the visualization and experimental results have shown the effectiveness of our attack method to figure out the limitations of medical diagnosis systems for further improvement.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a timely and significant problem: the lack of consistency in adversarial attacks when applied across the diverse, noisy, and specialized modalities of medical imaging. The authors provide a clear motivation, correctly identifying that standard techniques—like FGSM or PGD—fail to adapt to the inherent variance between subjects such as fundus cameras and CT scanners. The introduction is well-structured, effectively using Figure 1 to demonstrate the limitations of existing methods and framing the "Stabilized Medical Image Attack" (SMIA) as a necessary evolution for ensuring the robustness of Clinical Aided Diagnosis (CADx) systems. However, the manuscript’s strength hinges on the novelty of the "loss stabilization term"; while the authors claim this term helps "exhaustively search" for optima and mitigate noise, the mathematical justification provided in the text is currently dense. To elevate the paper, the authors should ensure that the transition between the empirical observation of "inconsistent attack results" and the theoretical formulation of the stabilization loss is more fluid, as the link between Gaussian smoothing and local optimum avoidance is the core technical contribution that warrants more rigorous conceptual unpacking.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the interpretation of the stabilization term
comments.quote: Specifically, we maximize the first term while minimizing the second term in Eq. 8. The maximization of the first term resembles existing adversarial attack methods (e.g., FGSM and PGD). However, the minimization of the second term consists of minimizing both $\eta^{\prime}$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_{1,i}^2$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors derive a stabilization objective from the KL-divergence, leading to Eq. 8. When discussing the minimization of the second term (the stabilization regularization), they state that the objective is to minimize $\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i^2$, and claim a result requiring $p_1 = p_2 = ... = p_K = 1/K$. However, mathematically, for a probability distribution vector where $\sum p_i = 1$, the sum of squares $\sum p_i^2$ is minimized at the uniform distribution ($1/K, ..., 1/K$), but if they are minimizing the trace of the Fisher Information Matrix (often related to the inverse of uncertainty or variance), the optimization usually involves maximizing entropy or minimizing information gain. The prose claim that the stabilization forces the prediction toward a uniform distribution ($[1/K, ..., 1/K]$) is theoretically unusual for adversarial attacks, which typically aim to force the model towards a specific incorrect class, not a high-entropy state. Furthermore, the notation $p_{1,i}$ is undefined; earlier, the authors define $p_j(x)$ as the probability of category $j$. The index '1' in $p_{1,i}$ is ambiguous—it likely refers to the first prediction or a specific component, but it contradicts the previous indexing.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Gaussian noise kernel application
comments.quote: where $W$ is a Gaussian kernel and convolves with the current perturbation $\eta$.
comments.explanation: The authors define the stabilization term $L_{STA}$ using $W \ast \eta - \eta$. In the section describing the stabilization term, they note it constrains $f(x+\eta)$ and $f(x+W \ast \eta)$ to be similar. It is standard in adversarial literature to define the 'smoothed' input as $x + \tilde{\eta}$ where $\tilde{\eta}$ is the smoothed perturbation. However, the formulation $W \ast \eta$ alone without clarifying the boundary conditions (padding) or the kernel size (e.g., standard deviation) makes the implementation underspecified. While this is an ICLR paper, the specific 'searching' property claimed (exhaustively searching the perturbation space) relies entirely on the choice of $W$, which is never characterized.

Given this is the foundational step before the stabilization term is introduced, the missing formula prevents an exact understanding of whether the initial step is a standard PGD/FGSM step or something different.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: definition of Fisher Information Matrix
comments.quote: G_x ˜ = E_y [( ∇x ˜ log p( y|x ))( ∇x ˜ log p( y|x ))[T] ]
comments.explanation: In the definition of the Fisher Information Matrix (G), the authors use the gradient with respect to the input x_tilde (∇x_tilde) but then express it as log p(y|x). Given that x_tilde is the adversarial example x + eta, it is likely the authors meant ∇x_tilde log p(y|x_tilde). While contextually clear, the inconsistency between the subscript of the gradient and the variable inside the log function is technically imprecise in a formal derivation context.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Jacobian/Fisher formulation
comments.quote: G_f is the Fisher Information Matrix of f(˜x) and the term J is the Jacobian matrix which can be computed as J = ∂ ∂f x ˜.
comments.explanation: The Jacobian is defined as partial derivative of f with respect to x_tilde. Mathematically, J = ∂f/∂x_tilde. The transcription presents this as '∂ ∂f x ˜', which is non-standard and garbled. Furthermore, the relationship provided (G_x = J^T G_f J) assumes a specific chain rule application that is standard in natural language processing/attack literature (like the Zhao et al. reference), but because the dimensions of J are (K x D) where D is the input dimension, the multiplication order presented in the paper (J^T G_f J) is dimensionally inconsistent if G_f is (K x K). Usually, G_x (D x D) = J^T (D x K) * G_f (K x K) * J (K x D). The text appears to have reversed the Jacobian or mislabeled the dimensions.

comments.title: Questionable claim regarding global optimum of trace minimization
comments.quote: the optimal solution to min[ K i =1 p 1 i, s. t. K i =1 p[i] = 1] is p 1 = p 2 = ... = pK = K 1.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that minimizing the stabilization term forces the CNN predictions toward a uniform distribution (1/K). While this is a plausible interpretation of 'smoothing', it suggests that the adversarial attack intends to make the classifier maximally uncertain (uniform prediction). However, adversarial attacks typically aim to push predictions to an *incorrect* class (targeting a specific class) or maximizing loss. The logic that 'consistent loss' implies a 'uniform probability distribution' is a leap that isn't fully justified; a consistent output could be a fixed *wrong* prediction, not necessarily a uniform one.

comments.title: Inconsistent formulation of the objective function minimization
comments.quote: Specifically, we maximize the first term while minimizing the second term in Eq. 8. The maximization of the first term resembles existing adversarial attack methods (e.g., FGSM and PGD). However, the minimization of the second term consists of minimizing both $\eta'$ and K $\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i(1-p_i)$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors claim that minimizing the stabilization term involves minimizing both the perturbation variance ($\eta'$) and the Fisher information (the trace term). However, the trace of $G_f$ is $\sum p_i(1-p_i)$. The text incorrectly writes this as $K \sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i(1-p_i)$ or suggests a factor related to $K$ in front of the sum. Furthermore, the goal is to drive $p_i$ to $1/K$ (uniform distribution). If $p_i = 1/K$, then $p_i(1-p_i) = (1/K)(1 - 1/K) = (K-1)/K^2$. The sum $\sum_{i=1}^K p_i(1-p_i)$ would thus be $\sum_{i=1}^K \frac{K-1}{K^2} = K \frac{K-1}{K^2} = \frac{K-1}{K} = 1 - 1/K$. The authors' stated intuition about the 'fixed objective spot' $1/K$ is correct, but the expression of the trace term and the role of $K$ in the expansion is mathematically imprecise, potentially confusing a reader trying to verify the derivation.

comments.title: Ambiguous variable definition for the loss stabilization term
comments.quote: $L_{STA} \approx \text{Tr}(G_f) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i(1-p_i)$
comments.explanation: In the summary and earlier sections, the authors define $L_{STA}$ using the Fisher Information Matrix trace. However, in Equation 3, $L_{STA}$ is defined as a loss function measuring the difference between $f(x+\eta)$ and $f(x+W*\eta)$. While the authors argue these are 'approximately' the same by Taylor expansion, the leap from the pixel-space difference of predictions to the Fisher information trace of the model output is a significant jump. The notation $p_i(1-p_i)$ implies a specific categorical loss structure (cross-entropy), but this is not explicitly stated as a global constraint on the loss function $L$ for all tasks (segmentation, detection, classification) where $p_i$ might represent pixel-wise probabilities or detection heatmaps, making the derivation potentially inconsistent across the reported modalities.

comments.title: Table 1 metric column mismatch

For example, 'Acc(%)_↓_Jaccard(%)_↓_Cos(%)_↑_' is listed as a single header for the 'COVID-19' column, but the data rows underneath appear to contain distinct values for these metrics. While the content is legible, the structural formatting creates ambiguity regarding which specific metric corresponds to which row/data point, especially as the number of values in the header string does not match the number of columns indicated by the pipes.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in KL-divergence expansion
comments.quote:
KL-divergence ... can be expanded via the second-order Taylor expansion (Shen et al., 2019) as follows:

$D_{KL} \approx \frac{1}{2} \eta'^T G_{x'} \eta'$
comments.explanation: The text states the objective is to minimize $D_{KL}$ between $f(\tilde{x})$ and $f(\tilde{x} + \eta')$. If the distribution is approximated by a second-order Taylor expansion of a KL-divergence centered at the current model output, standard information geometry (and the referenced logic from Zhao et al./Shen et al.) dictates that the second-order term involves the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). However, the formula $\frac{1}{2} \eta'^T G_{x'} \eta'$ represents the movement of the input in the manifold; the authors subsequently state this equals $\text{Tr}(G_f)$. This is only strictly true if the input perturbation $\eta'$ acts as a white noise process across all dimensions or if specific assumptions about the distribution of $\eta'$ (e.g., identity covariance) are met. While the authors define $G_{x'} = J^T G_f J$, the step equating the quadratic form to the trace of the FIM requires an expectation over the perturbation $\eta'$ that is not explicitly stated, potentially confusing a reader who expects the quadratic form to remain dependent on the specific direction of $\eta'$.

comments.title: Ambiguity in loss stabilization objective derivation
comments.quote: the optimal solution to $\min \sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i(1-p_i)$ s.t. $\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i = 1$ is $p_1 = p_2 = \dots = p_K = 1/K$
comments.explanation: The authors claim that minimizing the stabilization term $\text{Tr}(G_f) = \sum p_i(1-p_i)$ encourages the distribution to become uniform. Mathematically, $\sum p_i(1-p_i) = \sum p_i - \sum p_i^2 = 1 - \sum p_i^2$. Minimizing $1 - \sum p_i^2$ is equivalent to maximizing $\sum p_i^2$. The maximum of $\sum p_i^2$ subject to $\sum p_i = 1$ is achieved at a one-hot distribution (where one $p_i = 1$ and others are $0$), not the uniform distribution. The uniform distribution (where all $p_i = 1/K$) actually *maximizes* the variance $\sum p_i(1-p_i)$ (or minimizes $\sum p_i^2$). This appears to be a fundamental contradiction between their derivation and their stated goal of 'smoothing' or 'constant vector' target.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses a significant gap in adversarial robustness research by targeting the inconsistency of attacks across heterogeneous medical imaging modalities, where standard gradients often fail due to domain-specific noise and structural variance. The proposed stabilization mechanism, which leverages Gaussian smoothing to regularize the perturbation update trajectory and escape local optima, is conceptually sound and well-motivated by the empirical failures of FGSM and PGD shown in Figure 1; however, the presentation suffers from notable clarity issues, including ambiguous terminology (e.g., "smooth the single spot" lacks technical precision) and an abrupt truncation of the text that prevents evaluation of the mathematical derivation and experimental conclusions. The most significant concerns lie in the positioning of the novelty: the "stabilization term" closely resembles consistency regularization or gradient smoothing techniques used in adversarial training, and the authors must rigorously demonstrate that this modification yields superior attack efficacy (e.g., higher Attack Success Rate or faster convergence) rather than merely trading effectiveness for robustness, as the abstract's emphasis on "stability" and "small variance" risks obscuring the primary goal of generating effective adversarial examples. Additionally, the reliance on "KL-divergence analysis" to justify the method requires immediate elaboration in the text to substantiate the claim that the perturbation updates align with a fixed objective spot, and the manuscript must be completed to allow for a proper assessment of the benchmarks and statistical significance of the results.

comments.title: Terminological inaccuracy: 'Project Gradient Descent'
comments.quote: Project Gradient Descent (PGD) (Madry et al., 2017)
comments.explanation: I initially noticed the omission of the suffix '-ed' in the algorithm name. In the adversarial machine learning literature, this iterative method is universally known as 'Projected Gradient Descent' (Madry et al., 2017). The authors' intent is clearly to reference this standard attack, and the citation confirms it. However, dropping the suffix is a notable terminology error that should be corrected for academic precision. It does not affect the technical understanding but detracts from standard nomenclature.

comments.title: Missing closing parenthesis in citation list
comments.quote: Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) (Goodfellow et al., 2014) and Project Gradient Descent (PGD) (Madry et al., 2017), which are insufficiently developed
comments.explanation: The sentence introduces a list of methods with inline citations, but the parenthesis opened for the PGD reference is never closed before the relative clause 'which are...'. This appears to be a typesetting or OCR artifact. While the grammatical structure is slightly broken, the meaning remains clear: the cited methods are insufficiently developed for medical data. A simple punctuation fix would resolve it.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding optimization intuition
comments.quote: the proposed loss stabilization term exhaustively searches the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'smooth the single spot' initially confused me as it is not standard terminology in optimization or adversarial attack literature. I checked the later sections in the provided context (Section 3.2, KL-divergence analysis) to see if the intent was clarified. There, the authors explain that minimizing the stabilization loss reduces perturbation variance and steers updates toward a fixed direction, effectively smoothing out erratic updates to escape local optima. comments.title: Non-standard phrasing regarding clinical impact
comments.quote: Inaccurate diagnosis results make a negative influence on human healthcare.
comments.explanation: The collocation 'make a negative influence' is non-standard in academic English. The intended meaning is clearly that misdiagnosis adversely affects patient outcomes or healthcare systems. I checked the surrounding text, and the authors correctly establish the motivation for robustifying CADx systems. The technical claim is sound, but the phrasing should be adjusted to 'have a negative impact' or 'adversely affect' to meet standard academic conventions.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing: 'smooth the single spot'
comments.quote: The intuition from the scale space optimization (Lindeberg, 1992) indicates that the minimization of f ( A ) and f ( A [′] ) will exhaustively search the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'smooth the single spot' is highly ambiguous and likely an OCR artifact or translation error. In the context of scale-space theory and adversarial optimization, the authors intend to convey that constraining predictions across smoothed scales forces the optimization to find perturbations that are stable across scales, thereby escaping sharp, noise-sensitive local optima. 'Single spot' does not align with standard optimization or scale-space terminology; it likely should read 'optimization trajectory', 'search path', or 'stable region'. As written, a careful reader might incorrectly assume the method is smoothing a specific point in the image space rather than stabilizing the perturbation update direction.

comments.quote: However, the minimization of the second term consists of minimizing both _η[′]_ and[] _[K] i_ =1 _p_ 1 _i_[.] When we minimize _η[′]_ , we reduce the variance of the generated perturbation _η_ . On the other hand, the optimal solution to min[] _[K] i_ =1 _p_ 1 _i[,]_[ s] _[.]_[ t] _[.]_[ ] _i[K]_ =1 _[p][i]_[=][1][is] _[p]_[1][=] _[p]_[2][=] _[...]_[=] _[p][K]_[=] _K_ 1[.
comments.explanation: The text claims that minimizing the sum of predicted class probabilities $\sum_{i=1}^K p_i(\tilde{x})$ drives the output distribution toward uniformity ($p_i = 1/K$). However, for any valid softmax output of a CNN, $\sum_{i=1}^K p_i(\tilde{x}) \equiv 1$ at every iteration. Minimizing a constant subject to the constraint $\sum p_i = 1$ is mathematically undefined and trivial; it cannot yield the claimed optimum. The authors likely intended to minimize a different quantity, such as the cross-entropy loss $-\sum y_i \log p_i$, the negative entropy $-\sum p_i \log p_i$, or the sum of squared probabilities $\sum p_i^2$. This error fundamentally undermines the theoretical justification for how the stabilization term steers predictions toward a 'fixed objective spot,' as the stated objective cannot actually be optimized.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for iterative adversarial example update
comments.quote: When we iteratively add _[∂][L] ∂_[SMIA] _x_ ˜ to the last adversarial example _x_ , we are maximizing the values of _L_ SMIA.
comments.explanation: Throughout Section 3.1, $x$ is strictly defined as the original clean input image, while $\tilde{x} = x + \eta$ denotes the current adversarial example. Here, the authors refer to the 'last adversarial example' as $x$, which directly contradicts the established notation. This should correctly refer to $\tilde{x}_{t-1}$ or use a time-indexed notation to distinguish between the clean image and the sequentially updated adversarial example. This slip could mislead readers tracking the iterative update rule or attempting to reproduce the method.

comments.title: Mathematical imprecision equating trace minimization to eigenvalue minimization
comments.quote: As the trace of a matrix equals the summation of its eigenvalues which are all positive, minimizing eigenvalues is equivalent to finding the smallest trace.
comments.explanation: Minimizing the trace (the sum of eigenvalues) is not strictly equivalent to minimizing the eigenvalues themselves. A reduction in trace only guarantees that the *sum* of the eigenvalues decreases, not that each individual eigenvalue is minimized. While trace minimization is a widely used computationally tractable proxy in optimization literature, stating mathematical equivalence is inaccurate. The justification would be stronger if it explicitly framed trace minimization as a surrogate objective or an upper-bound relaxation for controlling the spectral norm or FIM magnitude.

comments.title: Contradiction between stated iteration limit and Table 2 results
comments.quote: We stop at the 10-th iteration for all the attack methods.
comments.explanation: Initially, I flagged the explicit statement that all attacks stop at 10 iterations, as it seemed to directly contradict Table 2, which presents results for 15, 20, and 25 iterations. To resolve this, I checked whether the authors explicitly separated the main experimental protocol from an ablation study. The text does not make this distinction, nor does it explain why Table 2 deviates from the stated stopping condition. A careful reader could reasonably conclude that either the methodology description is incomplete or the table is mislabeled. The authors should explicitly clarify that Table 2 is a hyperparameter analysis extending beyond the standard 10-iteration evaluation used in the main comparison.

comments.title: Misuse of 'cosine distance' when describing directional consistency
comments.quote: we measure the cosine distance between two consecutive perturbations to calculate the similarity of their directions... We also calculate the percentage of perturbations with a positive cosine distance since a positive value indicates that the directions of perturbation update are consistent during the iterative generation process.
comments.explanation: I was initially confused by the claim that a 'positive cosine distance' indicates consistent directions. To verify this, I recalled standard metric definitions: cosine distance is $1 - \cos(\theta)$ (range [0, 2]), where larger values mean *less* consistency, while cosine similarity is $\cos(\theta)$ (range [-1, 1]), where positive values indicate consistency. The authors correctly describe the behavior of cosine similarity but repeatedly label it as 'cosine distance'. I checked the surrounding context for non-standard definitions but found none. This terminology conflation is problematic because it could cause readers to invert their interpretation of the reported consistency percentages. The fix is to rename the metric to 'cosine similarity' or adjust the threshold description to 'distance < 1'.

comments.title: Table 1 values appear to contradict the stated attack premise
comments.explanation: When parsing the OCR-extracted Table 1 for the COVID-19 task, the values read as: Clean Accuracy = 87.82, DEV Accuracy = 93.57; Clean Jaccard = 69.16, DEV Jaccard = 83.66. The text immediately following claims that 'perturbations generated via deviation loss term (DEV) decrease the recognition performance'. As extracted, the DEV method actually *increases* both metrics, which contradicts the adversarial attack premise and the authors' own claims. I first suspected an OCR/layout artifact due to the messy table formatting, which is highly plausible. However, even assuming a plausible row alignment, the numerical discrepancy remains severe enough to warrant verification. If DEV genuinely improves performance on this segmentation task, it requires an explanation (e.g., noise acting as regularization); if it is a typo, the table needs correction to avoid misleading reproducibility attempts.

comments.title: Unreferenced MNIST baseline results (Table 5)
comments.quote: |Table 5: Numerical evaluations on the MNIST dataset. |Methods Accuracy(%) Decrease(%) Clean 99.39 - FGSM 75.90 23.63 PGD 74.88 24.66 SMIA 73.72 25.83|
comments.explanation: I noticed Table 5 abruptly presents attack performance on the standard MNIST digit dataset without any introductory text, justification, or discussion. To resolve this, I scanned the preceding and following paragraphs for references to natural images or MNIST, but found none. The paper's scope is explicitly medical, and the claims focus on cross-modality robustness across CT, fundus, and endoscopic data. While natural-image baselines are standard practice in adversarial literature, their sudden appearance mid-section without context is underspecified. A reader cannot determine if these results support a specific claim, serve as a sanity check, or are leftover draft material. The authors should either integrate it into the narrative or remove it to maintain focus on the medical imaging contributions.

comments.title: Terminology shift: 'CNN feature space' vs 'perturbation/attack space'
comments.quote: The visualization accords with the KL-divergence analysis that the proposed loss stabilization term decreases the perturbation variance and constrain a stable update direction towards a fixed objective spot in the CNN feature space.
comments.explanation: I initially wondered why the trajectory was suddenly placed in the 'CNN feature space', as the KL-divergence derivation and perturbation updates clearly operate on input gradients $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x$. To resolve this, I checked prior usage: the authors consistently refer to the 'attack space' or 'perturbation space' when discussing the trajectory of $\eta$. In deep learning, 'feature space' refers to intermediate layer activations, while perturbations are added to the input. Since SMIA's optimization is gradient-based on the input, the trajectory resides in the input/perturbation space, not hidden features. This phrasing could mislead readers into believing the stabilization directly constrains intermediate representations. I flagged it as minor because the mathematical derivation remains sound, but the geometric intuition is slightly mislabeled.

comments.title: Inconsistent objective function formulation and optimization direction
comments.quote: The proposed method iteratively generates adversarial perturbations by maximizing the deviation loss term and minimizing the loss stabilization term.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned by the optimization direction stated in the Conclusion. The summary and Section 3.1 formally define the objective function as a sum: $\mathcal{L}_{SMIA} = \mathcal{L}_{DEV} + \alpha \mathcal{L}_{STA}$. However, the Conclusion (and Section 3.2) explicitly states that the perturbation update is performed by 'maximizing the deviation loss term and minimizing the loss stabilization term.' Mathematically, performing gradient ascent on a positive linear combination would increase both terms simultaneously, not move them in opposite directions. While Section 3.2 hints that Eq. 8 reformulates this as a maximization of one term and minimization of another (implying a minus sign), this contradicts the explicit $+$ sign in the original objective definition. This creates a reproducibility gap: a reader would not know whether to add or subtract the stabilization term when computing $\eta = \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\partial \mathcal{L}_{SMIA} / \partial x)$. The issue is fixable by clarifying the sign in the objective or explicitly describing a bi-level/multi-objective update scheme.

comments.title: Ambiguous comparison of IoU values versus performance drops
comments.quote: Nevertheless, its performance decrease is not as significant as ours (i.e., 7.62% v.s. 4.8%) under the IoU metric that measures the bounding box overlap ratios.
comments.explanation: On first read, this sentence appears to claim that the authors' performance decrease (4.8%) is smaller than DAG's (7.62%), which would contradict their earlier statement that SMIA achieves a larger drop. I checked Table 6 and verified that 7.62% and 4.8% are actually the absolute IoU scores, not the deltas from the clean baseline (22.86%). The authors intended to say that SMIA achieves a lower absolute IoU (4.8% < 7.62%), implying a larger drop. However, phrasing it as 'performance decrease... (i.e., 7.62% v.s. 4.8%)' conflates raw metric values with calculated drops. While a careful reader can resolve this by cross-referencing the table, the wording momentarily creates a logical contradiction that could mislead those who don't check the table immediately.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses a significant gap in adversarial robustness research by targeting the inconsistency of attacks across heterogeneous medical imaging modalities, where standard gradients often fail due to domain-specific noise and structural variance. The proposed stabilization mechanism, which leverages Gaussian smoothing to regularize the perturbation update trajectory and escape local optima, is conceptually sound and well-motivated by the empirical failures of FGSM and PGD shown in Figure 1; however, the presentation suffers from notable clarity issues, including ambiguous terminology (e.g., "smooth the single spot" lacks technical precision) and an abrupt truncation of the text that prevents evaluation of the mathematical derivation and experimental conclusions. The most significant concerns lie in the positioning of the novelty: the "stabilization term" closely resembles consistency regularization or gradient smoothing techniques used in adversarial training, and the authors must rigorously demonstrate that this modification yields superior attack efficacy (e.g., higher Attack Success Rate or faster convergence) rather than merely trading effectiveness for robustness, as the abstract's emphasis on "stability" and "small variance" risks obscuring the primary goal of generating effective adversarial examples. Additionally, the reliance on "KL-divergence analysis" to justify the method requires immediate elaboration in the text to substantiate the claim that the perturbation updates align with a fixed objective spot, and the manuscript must be completed to allow for a proper assessment of the benchmarks and statistical significance of the results.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-level Feedback

This paper addresses an important and timely problem—adversarial robustness in medical imaging—and the proposed stabilization term is a conceptually interesting contribution. However, the paper's clarity and framing suffer from several significant issues that undermine its impact. First, the motivation conflates two distinct problems: intentionally malicious attacks and naturally occurring image noise. The paper's claim that Gaussian smoothing helps escape "local optima caused by image noise" is theoretically underdeveloped; the relationship between smoothing, the loss landscape, and the "fixed objective spot" referenced in the abstract is never clearly formalized. Second, the experimental setup is problematic: comparing against only FGSM and PGD—both single-step or simple iterative methods—is insufficient to demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, and no defense-aware or adaptive attack evaluation is conducted. Third, the paper lacks rigorous statistical analysis (e.g., confidence intervals, significance tests) across multiple runs, which is especially critical for medical applications where perturbation variance and attack stability are central claims. Finally, the writing is at times imprecise (e.g., "exhaustively searches the perturbation space" is misleading for an iterative gradient-based method), and the figure caption fails to explain why PGD's apparent success on the CT slice is not considered a "consistent" attack. These issues collectively weaken what could otherwise be a valuable contribution to medical adversarial robustness.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the description of loss stabilization term application across iterations
comments.quote: To tackle the instability problem, we introduce the loss stabilization term together with the loss deviation to form the new objective function, starting from the second iteration of the training process. We compute η in the first iteration using Eq. 2.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the stabilization term is introduced starting from the second iteration, and that the first iteration uses only Eq. 2 (the deviation term). This is a deliberate design choice. However, the earlier general description in the introduction and abstract says the objective function 'consists of a loss deviation term and a loss stabilization term' without qualifying that it is only used from iteration 2 onward. This could mislead a careful reader into thinking the stabilization term is present from the start. The context later clarifies it, but the initial framing is slightly imprecise. This is a minor framing concern.

comments.title: Incomplete and potentially erroneous derivation of the final objective transformation to Eq. 8 and the claim of uniform predictions
comments.quote: tr(G_f) = Σ (1/p_i) (∇_{f_i} p_i)^2 ... Therefore, the objective function shown in Eq. 4 can be formulated as follows: L_SMIA ≈ L_DEV - (λ/2) η'^T G_ẋ η' ... On the other hand, the optimal solution to min Σ 1/p_i s.t. Σ p_i=1 is p_i=1/K. This indicates that the stabilization loss term enforces the CNN prediction to update towards a constant vector [1/K,...,1/K].
comments.explanation: The derivation jumps from the trace of G_f (which involves gradients of probabilities) to a simplified expression involving Σ 1/p_i, but the full derivation of how this relates to η'^T G_ẋ η' is not shown. The authors reference Zhao et al. (2019) for the relation G_ẋ = J^T G_f J, but the trace expression given for G_f is not a standard canonical form. In standard Fisher Information for a categorical distribution with softmax outputs, the Fisher Information matrix element is [G_f]_{ij} = E[(∂ log p / ∂ f_i)(∂ log p / ∂ f_j)] = p_i(δ_{ij} - p_j), not (1/p_i)(∇_{f_i} p_i)^2. The derivation appears to contain an error or oversimplification. Furthermore, the claim that the stabilization loss term 'enforces the CNN prediction to update towards' a uniform distribution is a strong statement that requires justification. The objective function L_SMIA maximizes the deviation term while minimizing the stabilization term; it is not immediately clear that the stabilization term alone would push toward uniform predictions, especially when the deviation term is simultaneously pulling predictions away from the true label. The conclusion overstates what the analysis shows.

comments.title: Questionable claim about the effect of stabilization term on perturbation direction
comments.quote: This indicates that the stabilization loss term enforces the CNN prediction to update towards a constant vector [1/K, 1/K, ..., 1/K]. To this end, the perturbations are learned to move consistently towards a fixed objective spot during different iterations in the CNN attack space. As this spot is not related to network structure nor data type, our loss stabilization term improves perturbation robustness that overcomes huge variations brought by different networks and different types of input data.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the stabilization term drives CNN predictions toward a uniform distribution (1/K for each class), which is a fixed spot independent of the model or data. While mathematically, minimizing the trace of the Fisher Information (if the derivation were correct) might push towards a uniform distribution, the reasoning conflates two things: (1) the effect on the Fisher Information eigenvalues and (2) the actual update direction of the perturbation. The perturbation update is determined by the gradient of the full objective, not just the trace term. The claim that perturbations are updated 'towards a fixed objective spot' is vague. It is true that if the CNN predictions become uniform, the gradient of the cross-entropy loss becomes zero for all classes, but the authors are arguing about the Fisher term, not the cross-entropy. This overstates the interpretability of the optimization. The claim that this 'overcomes huge variations' is a qualitative assertion not directly proven by the analysis. This is a moderate overclaim.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the objective function sign convention for maximization vs. minimization
comments.quote: L_SMIA = L_DEV - (λ/2) η'^T G_ẋ η' ... When we iteratively add ∂L_SMIA/∂ẋ to the last adversarial example ẋ, we are maximizing the values of L_SMIA. Specifically, we maximize the first term while minimizing the second term. ... The proposed method iteratively generates adversarial perturbations by maximizing the deviation loss term and minimizing the loss stabilization term.
comments.explanation: The authors state that maximizing L_SMIA maximizes the deviation term and minimizes the stabilization term (because it appears with a negative sign). However, the sign convention could be confusing: in Eq. 4, the objective function is defined as L_SMIA = L_DEV + α L_STA with L_STA = ℒ(f(ẋ), f(ẋ+η')). Normally, the loss ℒ is a cross-entropy that is positive, and one would want to maximize the difference between predictions, so L_DEV is likely a cross-entropy that is maximized. The stabilization term L_STA is typically a divergence that is minimized to make predictions similar. But if both are added, maximizing the sum could conflict. The authors later clarify via KL-divergence that the stabilization term effectively appears with a negative sign in the expansion. This is a non-trivial detail that could confuse readers. The explanation is not fully aligned between the original objective (sum) and the expanded form (difference). Additionally, the conclusion states that the stabilization term is minimized, which contradicts the formal equation unless α is negative (not specified) or the optimization is performed differently. This is a moderate underspecification.

comments.title: Impossible relationship between Jaccard index and pixel accuracy for COVID-19 lung segmentation
comments.explanation: The table reports for the COVID-19 lung segmentation task: Clean accuracy 87.82% and Jaccard index 93.57%. In segmentation, Jaccard (IoU) = TP/(TP+FP+FN) is always ≤ pixel accuracy = (TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN), with equality only when TN is zero (which is not realistic here). Having Jaccard > accuracy is mathematically impossible. The same pattern holds across all rows (DEV and DEV+STA), suggesting either the column labels are swapped (Jaccard is actually accuracy and vice versa) or the numbers are misreported. Since the metric definitions in the paper state 'mean accuracy and Jaccard index for segmentation' and the table header lists 'Acc(%)' first, the most likely intended reading is that accuracy should be 93.57% and Jaccard 87.82% for the clean case, but this is pure speculation. The error as presented undermines the credibility of the ablation results for this dataset.

comments.title: Inconsistent clean accuracy for APTOS-2019 between tables
comments.quote: |Clean 81.28 -
comments.explanation: In Table 1 of the summary, the clean accuracy for APTOS-2019 is 81.82, but in Table 6 of the passage it appears as 81.28. Both tables report the same baseline for other datasets, so this discrepancy suggests a data error or OCR misread. If the correct clean accuracy is 81.82, the reported drop from SMIA (57.37) is 24.45, and the text claims 'around 10% more than the second best method (i.e., PGD)'. Since the PGD accuracy (67.46) yields a drop of 14.36 (if clean=81.82), the difference is 10.09 points, which still supports the claim. However, the inconsistency undermines the trustworthiness of the numerical values in the comparison table and should be corrected.

comments.title: Ambiguous column assignment and values for Kaggle-DR in Table 6
comments.explanation: The header for Kaggle-DR reads 'Acc(%) Var FPR(%)', but the row values appear inconsistently ordered. For FGSM, the three numbers could be Acc=18.18, Var=58.91, FPR=0.064, which would make Var unrealistically large (~59) compared to other entries (≤0.1). For SMIA, the numbers (51.26, 0.041, 27.80) would then show Acc=51.26, Var=0.041, FPR=27.80, which is plausible. A more likely interpretation is that the columns are misaligned due to OCR: for each method, the first number is accuracy, the second is false positive rate (FPR), and the third is variance. Under that reading, FGSM would have FPR=58.91% (reasonable), and SMIA would have FPR=27.80% (consistent with the text claiming higher FPR for SMIA). However, the variance for FGSM would then be 0.064, which matches typical magnitudes. The ambiguity could mislead readers into misinterpreting which metric corresponds to which value. Since the original paper likely has a correct layout, the OCR-induced confusion should be flagged.

comments.explanation: The metric 'Var' is used to compare perturbation consistency across methods, but the paper does not define how it is computed (e.g., variance over pixel values of the final perturbation? Variance across iterations? Pooled variance?). Without a precise definition, a reader cannot reproduce the comparison or verify the claim that SMIA produces lower variance. While the visualization in Fig. 2 gives an intuitive sense, a formal definition is necessary for reproducibility.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-level Feedback

This paper addresses an important and timely problem—adversarial robustness in medical imaging—and the proposed stabilization term is a conceptually interesting contribution. However, the paper's clarity and framing suffer from several significant issues that undermine its impact. First, the motivation conflates two distinct problems: intentionally malicious attacks and naturally occurring image noise. The paper's claim that Gaussian smoothing helps escape "local optima caused by image noise" is theoretically underdeveloped; the relationship between smoothing, the loss landscape, and the "fixed objective spot" referenced in the abstract is never clearly formalized. Second, the experimental setup is problematic: comparing against only FGSM and PGD—both single-step or simple iterative methods—is insufficient to demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, and no defense-aware or adaptive attack evaluation is conducted. Third, the paper lacks rigorous statistical analysis (e.g., confidence intervals, significance tests) across multiple runs, which is especially critical for medical applications where perturbation variance and attack stability are central claims. Finally, the writing is at times imprecise (e.g., "exhaustively searches the perturbation space" is misleading for an iterative gradient-based method), and the figure caption fails to explain why PGD's apparent success on the CT slice is not considered a "consistent" attack. These issues collectively weaken what could otherwise be a valuable contribution to medical adversarial robustness.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in the definition of adversarial perturbation direction
comments.quote: The perturbation \( r \) can be computed by taking the partial derivatives of the objective function with respect to the input. It can be written as:
comments.explanation: The text describes computing the perturbation r as partial derivatives of the objective function, but the actual equation (Eq. 2) is omitted from the provided context. The authors then state "The perturbation is further refined to \( \eta = \epsilon\cdot \text{sign}(r) \)". In standard FGSM-style attacks, the sign of the gradient is used. However, without seeing Eq. 2, it is unclear whether r is defined as the raw gradient or something else. The notation ambiguity is minor because the surrounding prose and the introduction (mentioning FGSM) strongly imply r is the gradient, but a reader might wonder if r had been defined differently in the missing equation. This is a moderate issue only if the missing equation introduced a different definition; from context, it is almost certainly the gradient, so it is minor.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the description of loss stabilization term application across iterations
comments.quote: To tackle the instability problem, we introduce the loss stabilization term together with the loss deviation to form the new objective function, starting from the second iteration of the training process. We compute \(\eta\) in the first iteration using Eq. 2.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the stabilization term is introduced starting from the second iteration, and that the first iteration uses only Eq. 2 (the deviation term). This is a deliberate design choice. However, the earlier general description in the introduction and abstract says the objective function "consists of a loss deviation term and a loss stabilization term" without qualifying that it is only used from iteration 2 onward. This could mislead a careful reader into thinking the stabilization term is present from the start. The context later clarifies it, but the initial framing is slightly imprecise. This is a minor framing concern.

comments.title: Underspecified definition of the perturbation update in the stabilization term
comments.quote: where \(W\) is a Gaussian kernel and convolves with the current perturbation \(\eta\). This term enforces the CNN predictions of \(x + \eta\) and \(x + W * \eta\) similar under the current loss function.
comments.explanation: The text says the stabilization term is defined as \(L_{STA} = \mathcal{L}(f(\theta, \tilde{x}), f(\theta, \tilde{x} + \eta'))\) with \(\eta' = W * \eta - \eta\). This means the second input to the CNN is \(x + \eta + (W*\eta - \eta) = x + W*\eta\). That is clear. However, the phrase "convolves with the current perturbation \(\eta\)" could be ambiguous: does \(W * \eta\) mean a Gaussian blur applied to the perturbation image (same spatial dimensions) or is it a different operation? In image processing, convolving a kernel with an image is standard, so a reader would infer spatial convolution. This is fine. The underspecification is minor because the context of Gaussian smoothing is standard. No real issue.

comments.title: Potential sign error in the KL-divergence expansion or objective function formulation
comments.quote: \\(D_{KL}(f(\theta, \tilde{x}) \| f(\theta, \tilde{x} + \eta')) \approx \frac{1}{2} \eta'^T \mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}} \eta'\\)
comments.explanation: The KL-divergence expansion shown is a standard second-order Taylor expansion around the point where the two distributions are equal, with the first-order term vanishing because the KL divergence is minimized (zero) at that point. This is correct. However, this expansion holds only when \(\eta'\) is small and the distributions are close. The authors then argue that minimizing this term reduces variance of \(\eta'\). This is valid if the Fisher Information Matrix \(\mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}}\) is positive definite. The step is justified. A potential subtlety: the expansion uses the Fisher Information at \(\tilde{x}\), but the two arguments are \(\tilde{x}\) and \(\tilde{x}+\eta'\). The expansion is approximately correct if the difference is small. This is a minor technical point, but not an error.

comments.title: Incomplete derivation of the final objective transformation to Eq. 8
comments.quote: \\( \text{tr}(\mathbf{G}_f) = \sum_{i=1}^K \frac{1}{p_i} (\nabla_{f_i} p_i)^2 \\)  ... Therefore, the objective function shown in Eq. 4 can be formulated as follows: \\( L_{SMIA} \approx L_{DEV} - \frac{\lambda}{2} \eta'^T \mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}} \eta' \\)
comments.explanation: The derivation jumps from the trace of \(\mathbf{G}_f\) (which involves gradients of probabilities) to a simplified expression involving \(\sum 1/p_i\), but the full derivation of how this relates to \(\eta'^T \mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}} \eta'\) is not shown. The authors reference Zhao et al. (2019) for the relation \(\mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{G}_f \mathbf{J}\), but then the trace expression given for \(\mathbf{G}_f\) is not a standard canonical form. The expression \(\sum_{i=1}^K \frac{1}{p_i} (\nabla_{f_i} p_i)^2\) seems to be missing an expectation or a derivative with respect to the log-probabilities. In standard Fisher Information for a categorical distribution with softmax outputs, the Fisher Information matrix element is \( [\mathbf{G}_f]_{ij} = \mathbb{E}[ (\partial \log p / \partial f_i)(\partial \log p / \partial f_j) ] = p_i(\delta_{ij} - p_j)\), not \(\frac{1}{p_i}(\nabla_{f_i} p_i)^2\). The derivation appears to contain an error or an oversimplification. The authors then conclude that minimizing the trace corresponds to making all \(p_i = 1/K\). While the conclusion (uniform distribution) is a known property of entropy maximization, the trace expression given is not the standard Fisher trace for softmax outputs. This is a moderate technical error unless the authors intended a different parameterization (e.g., using log-probabilities directly). Without seeing the referenced papers or the full derivation, this step is insufficiently justified and potentially incorrect.

comments.title: Questionable claim about the effect of stabilization term on perturbation direction
comments.quote: This indicates that the stabilization loss term enforces the CNN prediction to update towards a constant vector [1/K, 1/K, ..., 1/K]. To this end, the perturbations are learned to move consistently towards a fixed objective spot during different iterations in the CNN attack space. As this spot is not related to network structure nor data type, our loss stabilization term improves perturbation robustness that overcomes huge variations brought by different networks and different types of input data.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the stabilization term drives CNN predictions toward a uniform distribution (1/K for each class), which is a fixed spot independent of the model or data. While mathematically, minimizing the trace of the Fisher Information (if the derivation were correct) might push towards a uniform distribution, the reasoning conflates two things: (1) the effect on the Fisher Information eigenvalues and (2) the actual update direction of the perturbation. The perturbation update is determined by the gradient of the full objective, not just the trace term. The claim that perturbations are updated "towards a fixed objective spot" is vague. It is true that if the CNN predictions become uniform, the gradient of the cross-entropy loss becomes zero for all classes (since the derivative of cross-entropy with respect to logits is p_i - y_i), but the authors are arguing about the Fisher term, not the cross-entropy. This overstates the interpretability of the optimization. The claim that this "overcomes huge variations" is a qualitative assertion not directly proven by the analysis. This is a moderate overclaim.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the objective function sign convention for maximization vs. minimization
comments.quote: \\(L_{SMIA} = L_{DEV} - \frac{\lambda}{2} \eta'^T \mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}} \eta'\\) ... When we iteratively add \(\frac{\partial L_{SMIA}}{\partial \tilde{x}}\) to the last adversarial example \(\tilde{x}\), we are maximizing the values of \(L_{SMIA}\). Specifically, we maximize the first term while minimizing the second term.
comments.explanation: The authors state that maximizing \(L_{SMIA}\) maximizes the deviation term and minimizes the stabilization term (because it appears with a negative sign). However, the stabilization term \(\eta'^T \mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}} \eta'\) is positive (since \(\mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}}\) is positive definite). So maximizing \(L_{SMIA}\) indeed reduces this quadratic term. That is consistent. But the sign convention could be confusing: in Eq. 4 (not shown), was the stabilization term added or subtracted? The description "maximizing the first term while minimizing the second term" suggests that the second term is subtracted. But in the earlier text (Section 3.1), the objective function is defined as \(L_{SMIA} = L_{DEV} + \alpha L_{STA}\) with \(L_{STA} = \mathcal{L}(f(\tilde{x}), f(\tilde{x}+\eta'))\). Normally, the loss \(\mathcal{L}\) is a cross-entropy that is positive, and one would want to maximize the difference between predictions, so \(L_{DEV}\) is likely a cross-entropy that is maximized (i.e., making predictions wrong). The stabilization term \(L_{STA}\) is typically a divergence that is minimized to make predictions similar. But if both are added, maximizing the sum could conflict. The authors later clarify via KL-divergence that the stabilization term effectively appears with a negative sign in the expansion. This is a non-trivial detail that could confuse readers. The explanation is not fully aligned between the original objective (sum) and the expanded form (difference). This is a moderate underspecification.

However, since this is a conference paper with a full bibliography elsewhere, this is a minor formatting issue.

comments.title: Overly strong claim about solving limitations via SMIA without evidence in passage
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the limitations that are brought by huge network and data variance are effectively solved via our stabilized medical attack.
comments.explanation: The sentence claims that the limitations of 'huge network and data variance' are 'effectively solved' by SMIA. At this point in the paper (end of Section 2.2, before the Proposed Method section), no experimental evidence or formal justification has been presented. The claim is too strong for this stage of the paper. While the paper later presents results, this statement in the introduction to Section 3 could mislead a reader into thinking the solution is already proven here. It would be better phrased as 'addressed' or 'mitigated' until the experiments are shown.
